# Questions in Livestream 64

Live Stream #64: A Flock of Horus Birds.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:12</a> | How do I respond to skeptics who say Abraham could not have met Philistine kings because the Philistines did not exist yet? | He treats the Philistine references in Genesis as retrojection: a later author uses familiar contemporary terms for earlier settings rather than claiming Philistines were there in the full later historical sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=792" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:12</a> | Is the wall at Karnak from Shoshenq's time inscribed with names like the Fields of Abraham and the Highlands of David? | He does not think so from a quick scan, but he explicitly says he did not do enough preparatory work to confirm or deny it and would want a deeper look at the toponym list. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:15</a> | What advice do you have for new believers looking for a church in a time of apostasy? | He recommends looking for a church with serious mentoring and discipleship, because many churches do better at baptism and worship culture than at deep teaching and formation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1400" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:20</a> | In your Mount Ebal video, did you make a mistake about the second scarab carrying the name of Ramesses? | Yes, he says he made that mistake, but argues the scarab is still identifiable as a Ramesside type by its style and published parallels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:12</a> | How do we know the Egyptians did not boil water to make it safe for drinking? | He says they certainly could boil water, but there is no evidence that they did so specifically for drinking water, and beer was a more practical processed drink in their context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=1842" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:42</a> | Why did Abram say Sarah was his sister, didn't Egyptian pharaohs sometimes practice incest, and was Sarah really his half-sister? | He says Abram lied out of fear, pharaonic incest happened mainly in succession-crisis contexts, and Sarah was likely a more distant clan relative than a literal half-sister. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:50</a> | If you could ask God one question in heaven, what would you ask? | He says he would ask for access to God's library. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2397" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:57</a> | What is the origin of the expression "sons of Belial"? | He says it originally means worthless people or sons without value, with later demonological readings developing in the Second Temple period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2567" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:47</a> | In Isaiah 45:13, does the grammar point to Cyrus rebuilding Jerusalem, and is there amphiboly or messianic layering in the verse? | He says the passage does involve ambiguity: Cyrus is in view, but he also sees a broader messianic fulfillment running through the theological discourse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=2931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:51</a> | How could Zerah the Ethiopian march against Asa if he would have had to pass through Egypt? | He argues Zerah was probably a famous Cushite general operating for Osorkon I, so the march would have happened with Egyptian approval, not against it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3195" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:15</a> | In 1 Kings 20:16, does Ben-hadad's coalition of 32 kings literally mean 32 major heads of state? | He says no; these were probably many small city-state rulers, so the number sounds bigger than the actual scale of each polity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:42</a> | Why did the Babylonian 60-based time system survive into the present day? | He says the picture is mixed: Egypt contributed the 24-hour day, Babylonia the 60-minute hour, and later Europeans applied the same geometrical logic to seconds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3677" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:17</a> | Do you think the Trojan War was a real event, and if so when and why did it happen? | Yes; he places it broadly around 1260 to 1180 BC and interprets it as agricultural and expansionary conflict tied to wider Late Bronze Age upheaval. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=3854" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:14</a> | Did ancient Near Eastern cultures think rival nations' gods were real, evil, or accurately described? | He says polytheistic cultures often accepted, equated, borrowed, or even stole other peoples' gods rather than simply treating them as evil fictions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:21</a> | In Judges, what verbal clues help tell whether judges ruled in succession or concurrently in different places? | He says context is the key, especially shifts in place, tribe, oppression, and the presence or absence of explicit temporal markers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4221" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:21</a> | What are your thoughts on the eruv wires around places like Manhattan? | He describes them as a creative legal workaround that uses the letter of later sabbath interpretation to create a symbolic enclosed area where carrying is allowed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:54</a> | Is dividing Old Testament laws into cultural, moral, and cultic categories a helpful method or just a trick to dismiss laws people dislike? | He thinks it is imperfect but legitimate when done with context, since not all laws function the same way and many were tied to temple practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=4774" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:34</a> | In Judges 1:7, were Adoni-bezek's seventy kings really heads of families or more like village rulers? | He says they were probably rulers of many small towns or villages, and some could even represent repeat conquests of the same places. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5071" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:24:31</a> | What were the grievous infirmities of Egypt in Deuteronomy 7:15? | He treats it as a broad reference to the severe disease environment of Egypt rather than one precisely identifiable illness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:02</a> | What is going on with Gideon's ephod in Judges 8:27? | He says Gideon makes a cultic ritual garment he should not be making, and once displayed it is treated like idolatrous apparatus and becomes a snare. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:14</a> | Are fortune cookies a form of divination, and should Christians avoid them? | He says no, not unless someone is treating them seriously as divination; ordinarily they are just playful sayings in cookies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=5912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:32</a> | In Exodus 25:9, is the tabernacle or its furniture just a scale model of a larger heavenly structure? | He does not find the simple scale-model reading convincing and thinks the sacred furniture also functioned as a real point of divine-human interface, not merely as a miniature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=6198" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:18</a> | Does Job 33:22-24 have christological overtones, and what should we make of Elihu's role there? | He says interpreters are all over the map and he does not want to force an opinion; the christological reading is possible, but he preserves uncertainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=6466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:46</a> | Is the story that someone found a golden ass image in the Jerusalem temple a fabrication, or does it connect to Seth or Hyksos traditions? | He thinks it is ideological fabrication and does not believe the Jerusalem temple would have normally housed that kind of animal image. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=6786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:06</a> | What is the best explanation for the discrepancy between Jesus' genealogies in Matthew and Luke? | He declines a firm answer because genealogies often serve non-obvious literary and theological purposes, and he has not studied those two closely enough yet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=7274" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:14</a> | Did Pharisees in the New Testament period believe their traditions came directly from Moses, and when did the traditions of the elders become the oral law? | He says the written oral law emerges in the post-70 AD rabbinic period, and Pharisees likely distinguished those traditions from the Torah itself rather than treating every elder tradition as direct Mosaic dictation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=7618" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:58</a> | What relationship would Moshe have had to Ramesses? Was it really brotherly? | He says no; Moses was probably older by about a decade and shared parts of the same household world, but not in the close brotherly way often depicted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=8160" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:00</a> | In Judges 8, why does Gideon kill the men of Penuel but not the men of Succoth when the offense seems similar? | He reads the difference as lying in the aftermath: Succoth seems to give up its leaders, while Penuel as a whole stands behind its resistance and tower, though he notes some speculation is involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=8772" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:12</a> | If I attend a local Messianic Jewish congregation, how do I avoid being "that Gentile"? | He says to behave normally and respectfully, avoid cosplay and overperforming Jewish identity, and be sensitive to congregational customs, especially around food. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=9453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:33</a> | Why is Pascha or Easter celebrated on Sunday rather than on a fixed calendar date? | He says Easter is tied to Sunday because of the resurrection, while different Christian traditions calculate the date differently in relation to the lunar calendar and Passover. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=9827" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:47</a> | If Moshe had not parted the Reed Sea, why could the Israelites not just go around the Yam Suph? | He says they were boxed in and could not outrun Pharaoh's chariots; the route itself functions as a divine trap for the Egyptian army. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:25</a> | Why do we not find chariots or evidence of them there after the crossing? | He says Egyptian chariots were built largely from wood and leather, so in that wet environment they would quickly fall apart and leave little to recover. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:50</a> | How deep and wide was the Sea of Reeds? | He estimates it was hundreds of miles wide and about 20 to 40 feet deep, based on sediment deposits and geological surveys in the area. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:54</a> | If the wind blew from the east, how would the sea have split, and would the people have had to fight hurricane-force winds? | He says the setdown effect would part the water roughly east to west and would not require hurricane winds, only about a strong 50 mph wind. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=10668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:57:48</a> | Why do Saul and David's world contain so many El and Baal names and fewer Yahwistic ones? | He says the period reflects widespread idolatry and cultural fashion, so those naming patterns fit a time when Israel was not consistently faithful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11006" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:26</a> | What resources are there for learning about continental young earth creationism? | He says he does not really have resources to recommend because that movement tends to be niche, local, and not strongly organized around publishing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:32</a> | Cheddar or mozzarella? | He says it depends entirely on the application, and neither is his overall favorite cheese. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:35</a> | When new dynasties took the throne, how did they establish religious legitimacy after starting as ordinary people? | He says they often linked themselves to royal ancestry, cults, king lists, or revered royal figures, though those legitimacy projects were not always fully successful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=11874" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:17:54</a> | Did biblical authors have room in their thinking for corporate or group persons? | Yes; he says clan and corporate responsibility are central to biblical thought, often more so than modern Western individualism expects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/1j6E3oI0fNk?t=12256" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:16</a> | How do I become a New Testament scholar coming out of high school? | He says to begin with a strong bachelor's program, get Greek early, and expect the long path of further graduate study and specialization. |
